student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Running and Following Status Meetings -- Reporting, Updating and Pushing Back
lesson.menu_desc: Chairing a status meeting with a late supplier: modal perfects, hedging and the diplomatic push back -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 3 -- Running and Following Status Meetings
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Lesson 3 -- Running and Following Status Meetings

listenings.text: João, this is Mei Lin Zhao from CASCO. I owe you an update after our call on Monday. First, the revised baseline schedule. It left engineering yesterday, and you will have it before Friday, exactly as I promised. Second, the inspection test plan. You were right to push back on it. I looked at the original requirement and I found that the hold points were removed by our documentation team, not by engineering. There must have been a misunderstanding when the template was updated in May. We are re-issuing the plan with all four hold points restored, and the corrective action is registered in our system. Third, and I want to flag this up before you hear it somewhere else. The factory acceptance test may have to move by one week, because the interlocking cabinet arrived from our own supplier with a damaged connector. It is a small non-conformance, and honestly, we should have caught it at goods-in. I did not want you to find that in an email. Talk to you on Friday.

Good morning. Before you dial into that call, three things about escalation, because I think we get this wrong as an organization. First. Escalation is not a punishment. If a supplier misses a date twice, and you have followed up on it in writing twice, escalation is simply the next step in the process. In most of our packages it should have happened weeks earlier. Second. Never escalate a problem you have not flagged up first. If the first time your counterpart hears about a non-conformance is from his own director, you have not managed the issue. You have ambushed him, and he will never help you again. Third. Escalate the issue, not the person. The corrective action was not implemented on time. That is a fact, and it belongs in the minutes. Their engineer is incompetent. That is an opinion, and it does not. One more thing, and then I will let you go. When they ask for a waiver, ask yourself what that waiver actually costs us on site. A waiver that saves the supplier three days in the factory can cost us three weeks during the site acceptance test. That is not a good deal, and you should push back on it.

modals.title: Same past. Four different messages.
cards: The duty existed and it was not done. You criticize the ACTION, never the person. "The ITP should have been submitted on June 3rd."
You are almost sure, from the evidence. It sounds analytical, not accusatory. "There must have been a bottleneck on your side."
You open a door and let the other person save face. "The drawings might have been sent to the wrong contact."
The evidence rules it out completely. "They could not have run the FAT without the cabinet."

modals.text: The modal you choose is a political decision, not a grammatical one. "You didn't send it" ends the meeting. "It should have been sent" starts the corrective action.

items.situation: "Good morning. You wanted to talk about the open items?" Open the meeting and raise the overdue schedule.
tips: Thanks for joining. I'd like to follow up on the revised baseline schedule.
It should have reached us on June 10th.

items.situation: "We sent the ITP two weeks ago. What is the issue?" The ITP has no hold points.
tips: I'd like to flag up an issue with the inspection test plan.
There are no hold points in it, only witness points.

items.situation: "Our team was waiting for YOUR interface document." It was signed in April. Push back.
tips: To some extent I understand your position, however the interface document was signed in April.
I have to push back on that.

items.situation: "The cabinet is damaged and we don't know why." Deduce the cause without accusing anyone.
tips: There must have been a problem in transport.
It might have been damaged before it left the factory.

items.situation: "Can you approve a waiver on the insulation test? It saves us three days." You are not convinced.
tips: One could argue that three days in the factory cost us three weeks on site.
I'm afraid I have to push back on that waiver.

items.situation: "Fine. What do you want from us, exactly?" Close the meeting with an agreement.
tips: Can we agree on a corrective action and a new deadline?
You re-issue the ITP with the hold points, and I'll send the minutes today.

bank.label: Opening and following up
items: Thanks for joining. Let's start with the open items.
I'd like to follow up on the revised baseline schedule.
Before we close, I'd like to flag up one more issue.

bank.label: Stating what went wrong
items: The inspection test plan should have been submitted by last Friday.
There must have been a miscommunication about the hold points.
The drawings might have been sent to the wrong contact.

bank.label: Disagreeing and pushing back
items: It would appear that we are not looking at the same revision.
One could argue that the baseline never reflected the real lead times.
To some extent I understand your position, however I have to push back on that date.

bank.label: Closing with an agreement
items: Can we reach a consensus on a corrective action and a new deadline?
I'll send the minutes today, and I'll follow up on Friday.
If the date slips again, I'm afraid escalation is the next step.
